title: AP Capacity Guide
summary: Understand client load, per-client throughput demand, airtime utilization, effective AP capacity, and practical clients-per-AP limits.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether the planned access point capacity can support the expected client count and bandwidth demand without pushing airtime too close to saturation.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document wireless design assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch RF, capacity, or margin pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning AP count, validating wireless capacity, reviewing high-density areas, or checking whether a coverage-first design also has enough usable throughput.
bullets: Early wireless design planning.
Expansion review or refresh planning.
Existing-system cleanup, validation, or troubleshooting support.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical wireless planning terms.

items.label: Total Clients
items.description: The total number of wireless clients expected in the area being planned.
items.whyItMatters: Client count drives shared airtime demand and determines whether AP count is a capacity problem instead of only a coverage problem.
items.commonMistake: Counting only laptops while ignoring phones, tablets, scanners, IoT devices, or guest devices.

links.label: Plan area density from client count

items.label: Avg Throughput per Client (Mbps)
items.description: The average usable throughput each active client is expected to need.
examples: Higher demand for video or file access
items.whyItMatters: Small per-client assumptions multiply quickly when many clients are active at the same time.
items.commonMistake: Using internet plan speed or AP headline speed as if every client can receive that rate.

items.label: Target Utilization (%)
items.description: The maximum AP airtime or capacity utilization you are comfortable designing around.
items.whyItMatters: A lower utilization target leaves room for retries, roaming, bursts, and client diversity.
items.commonMistake: Designing around 100% utilization and leaving no airtime margin.

items.label: Effective AP Capacity (Mbps)
items.description: The practical usable throughput expected from one AP after real-world overhead and client behavior are considered.
examples: Lower in noisy or crowded areas
items.whyItMatters: Effective capacity is what the AP can realistically deliver, not its advertised PHY rate.
items.commonMistake: Treating Wi-Fi 6 or Wi-Fi 7 marketing rates as sustained application throughput.

links.label: Check channel width and utilization impact

items.label: Max Clients per AP
items.description: The planning limit for how many clients one AP should support before adding more APs or changing the design.
items.whyItMatters: This prevents one AP from becoming the contention point even when signal strength looks acceptable.
items.commonMistake: Assuming client association limits are the same as good user experience.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means AP capacity appears to have practical margin. Watch means client count, utilization, or AP throughput assumptions should be reviewed. Risk means the design may need more APs, lower client expectations, or a different RF plan.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but RF conditions, client behavior, or operating margin should be reviewed.
Risk: add capacity, reduce assumptions, improve placement, or validate with a survey before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Wireless planning problems usually come from treating Wi-Fi like a fixed cable instead of a shared RF environment affected by airtime, interference, device capability, channel reuse, and roaming behavior.
bullets: Sizing only for coverage and ignoring capacity.
Using ideal vendor rates as if they are sustained usable throughput.
Ignoring noise floor, channel overlap, client mix, and airtime contention.
Failing to document assumptions before AP count, channel plan, or antenna placement is finalized.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace a wireless survey, manufacturer documentation, controller validation, spectrum analysis, or project-specific RF engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications and controller guidance still matter.
Client device capability can be the limiting factor even when AP hardware is strong.
Walls, mounting height, interference, antenna pattern, and channel reuse can change the final result.
A wireless design with margin is usually easier to support, tune, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal wireless design review, site survey validation, manufacturer guidance, or project-specific responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual AP model, antenna pattern, client mix, controller settings, and site survey results.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for coverage, speed, roaming, and reliability.
Document assumptions before implementation or final AP placement.
